primary_diagnosis.name: Severe symptomatic hypoglycaemia with neuroglycopenia (insulin-treated diabetes; mealtime insulin taken with skipped meal)

pathophysiology_summary: The patient injected his usual fast-acting (aspart) mealtime insulin but skipped the meal because of nausea, producing an insulin–carbohydrate mismatch. Unopposed exogenous insulin drove plasma glucose down, triggering first an autonomic (adrenergic) counter-regulatory response — diaphoresis, pallor, tremor, hunger, anxiety and tachycardia — and then, as glucose fell further, neuroglycopenia: diplopia, progressive confusion, reduced consciousness and loss of the ability to swallow or protect the airway. The bedside capillary glucose of 1.8 mmol/L confirms critical hypoglycaemia. Untreated, this progresses to seizure, coma, aspiration and death or permanent anoxic/hypoglycaemic brain injury. Definitive reversal is rapid glucose delivery: parenteral glucose or intramuscular glucagon (which mobilises hepatic glycogen) when the patient cannot safely swallow, followed by oral carbohydrate once consciousness and swallow return. Because short-acting insulin continues to act for hours, rebound hypoglycaemia is a real risk requiring ongoing monitoring.

differential.evidence_basis: Insulin-treated diabetic with abrupt adrenergic surge (sweating, tremor, tachycardia) progressing to altered mental status — classic hypoglycaemia until proven otherwise.
Acute confusion and diplopia can be stroke; but absence of focal/lateralising deficit and the profuse sympathetic diaphoresis point away from a vascular cause.
Anxiety, tremor and palpitations overlap with an adrenergic hypo, but cannot explain the objective critically low glucose or the fall in consciousness.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A capillary glucose check is the single highest-yield action; 'check the sugar' should be reflexive in any drowsy/confused person on insulin.

differential.evidence_basis: Whipple-type picture: low bedside glucose (1.8 mmol/L) + neuroglycopenic signs in a patient who took mealtime insulin and skipped food; adrenergic and neuroglycopenic features both present.

No focal or lateralising deficit; the documented critical hypoglycaemia fully explains the diplopia and confusion — hypoglycaemia is a recognised stroke mimic and must be corrected before any stroke pathway.
Sustained altered mental status and ongoing profuse diaphoresis without a situational trigger are not vasovagal.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Recognising hypoglycaemia as a stroke/psychiatric mimic — and correcting glucose FIRST — is the key reasoning step.

differential.evidence_basis: Confirmed critical low glucose with neuroglycopenia and airway-threatening obtundation; time-critical reversal required. No effective glucose delivered yet — the patient cannot swallow.

red_flags.description: Altered mental status / reduced consciousness with inability to swallow — neuroglycopenia threatening the airway
red_flags.clinical_significance: An obtunded patient who cannot protect the airway is at immediate risk of aspiration and progression to seizure or coma; oral treatment is unsafe and parenteral rescue plus 000 are required.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Aspiration, hypoglycaemic seizure, coma, anoxic brain injury or death.

red_flags.description: Critical capillary blood glucose (1.8 mmol/L) in an insulin-treated patient
red_flags.clinical_significance: Confirms severe hypoglycaemia; the depth of the low plus neuroglycopenic features defines a medical emergency.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to treat a rapidly reversible but lethal cause of coma.

red_flags.description: Mealtime (fast-acting) insulin taken with a skipped meal — ongoing insulin action
red_flags.clinical_significance: Short-acting insulin will keep driving glucose down for hours, so rebound hypoglycaemia is likely even after initial rescue — mandates ongoing monitoring and hospital assessment.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Premature reassurance and a second, unwitnessed collapse after apparent recovery.

red_flags.description: Cannot take oral carbohydrate — the usual self-treatment has failed
red_flags.clinical_significance: When oral rescue is impossible, only parenteral glucose or IM glucagon plus emergency services can reverse the hypoglycaemia.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Continued deterioration while ineffective oral measures are attempted.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on 'acute confusion + diplopia = stroke' and activating a stroke pathway without first checking and correcting glucose — hypoglycaemia is a classic, fully reversible stroke mimic.
Instructing the carer to give juice, glucose gel or food to an obtunded patient who cannot swallow — this risks aspiration and delays effective (parenteral) rescue.
Treating the episode as resolved once he wakes, without accounting for continuing short-acting insulin action and the resulting rebound-hypoglycaemia risk over the next several hours.
Trying to talk the family through home management instead of escalating: an obtunded, non-swallowing hypoglycaemic patient needs 000 and (if available) a glucagon rescue kit now.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Formal conscious-level assessment (GCS) and airway competence
Blood pressure, heart rate and temperature by clinician measurement
Laboratory plasma glucose, electrolytes and any point-of-care confirmation
Any parenteral treatment — IV dextrose or clinician-administered IM glucagon
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Call 000 immediately for an emergency ambulance; if an in-date glucagon rescue kit is in the home and a carer is willing/able, instruct its use into the outer thigh; place him on his side in the recovery position; give NOTHING by mouth until he is fully awake and can swallow; once awake and able to swallow, fast-acting then long-acting oral carbohydrate.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the diagnosis (bedside glucose plus classic constellation is definitive); the real risk is UNDER-action — deferring to morning review, attempting oral carbohydrate in an obtunded patient, or missing the rebound-hypoglycaemia risk.

guidelines_referenced.reference: ADS / Diabetes Australia — severe hypoglycaemia management
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: For hypoglycaemia with impaired consciousness or inability to swallow, do not give oral carbohydrate; give IM/SC glucagon or IV glucose and call emergency services; recheck glucose and give longer-acting carbohydrate once conscious.
Place an unresponsive but breathing casualty on their side (recovery position), keep the airway clear, give nothing by mouth, and call an ambulance.
